      Jane Street vs. Millennium
   =20
 =20
One sort of thing you could do, if you work at an investing firm, is say =
=E2=80=9CI like Microsoft Corp., I think this artificial intelligence thing=
 is big, I think the stock will go up.=E2=80=9D And then you buy Microsoft =
stock with your firm=E2=80=99s capital, and hopefully it goes up.Another so=
rt of thing you could do, if you work at an investing firm, is design a com=
plex software system that ingests millions of data points to find predictiv=
e signals and then uses those signals to make automated low-latency trades =
in multiple assets on multiple exchanges with limited human supervision. Yo=
u write thousands of lines of code, you test the system, you put it into pr=
oduction, it runs on its own and hopefully it makes money.=C2=A0And then on=
e day, as these things go, you might leave your investing firm and go work =
for another one. What can you bring with you? Well, if your big idea at you=
r old job was =E2=80=9Cwe should buy Microsoft,=E2=80=9D and you get to you=
r new job, and on the first day you walk in and say =E2=80=9CI like Microso=
ft, I think this artificial intelligence thing is big, I think the stock wi=

 In the event, Tesla=E2=80=99s market cap got as high as $1.2 trillion in 2=
021, at which point Musk=E2=80=99s option package was worth more than $100 =
billion. Tesla=E2=80=99s stock closed yesterday at $155.45, for a market ca=

n.

But at the time they were granted, in 2018, Musk could not extract any mone=

strike price of the options was set to be the same as Tesla=E2=80=99s stock=

Tesla maintained the status quo; they=E2=80=99d only be worth anything to M=

I have long been fond of a classic late-night dorm-room question of financi=
al capitalism, which is: =E2=80=9CWhat if people could sell stock in themse=
lves?=E2=80=9D Instead of borrowing money to go to college, you=E2=80=99d=
=C2=A0sell 10% of the equity in yourself to pay for college, and then when =
you graduated and got a good job you=E2=80=99d dividend out 10% of your inc=
ome to your shareholders. Everyone loves talking about this! Drink every ti=
me someone says the words =E2=80=9Cadverse selection.=E2=80=9DI sometimes g=
et hung up on a dumb technicality, though. You can=E2=80=99t=C2=A0really=C2=
=A0sell equity in yourself. You are not a corporation. You can do something=
 sort of similar, a rough economic equivalent. You can write a contract tha=
t says =E2=80=9CI will pay you 10% of my income forever,=E2=80=9D or whatev=
er the actual terms are. (Most of the practical=C2=A0proposals are more lik=
e =E2=80=9CA% of my income from B above a floor of $C=C2=A0for D=C2=A0years=
, with a lifetime cap of $E=E2=80=9D than they are =E2=80=9C10% of all my i=
ncome forever.=E2=80=9D) And this contract creates =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, I=
 think I would call it =E2=80=9Ca debt=E2=80=9D? It is a debt of indetermin=
ate size; it is a debt whose payments are conditional on future facts (your=
 income). But surely it is a=C2=A0debt. Surely if you=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t pa=
y, your =E2=80=9Cshareholders=E2=80=9D don=E2=80=99t go sue you in Delaware=
 Chancery Court for a breach of fiduciary duties. They sue you in regular c=
ourt for breach of contract, for not paying your debt.We talked a few years=
 ago about Lambda School, a coding academy that did not charge upfront tuit=
ion but entered into income-sharing agreements with its students, requiring=
 them to pay =E2=80=9C17% of their incomes for 24 months after landing a jo=
b that pays more than $50,000 a year,=E2=80=9D capped at $30,000.Yesterday =
the US Consumer Financial Protection Bureau settled a case against Lambda S=
chool=E2=80=99s successor, BloomTech, and its chief executive officer, Aust=
en Allred, =E2=80=9Cfor deceiving students about the cost of loans and maki=
ng false claims about graduates=E2=80=99 hiring rates.=E2=80=9D Much of thi=
s is standard consumer-finance stuff =E2=80=94=C2=A0BloomTech seems to have=
 put a sunny spin on its job-placement data =E2=80=94 but the part I found =
interesting was this:BloomTech falsely claimed its =E2=80=9Cincome share=E2=
=80=9D agreements were not loans, did not create debt, did not carry a fina=
nce charge, and were =E2=80=9Crisk free.=E2=80=9D In fact, the agreements a=
re loans with an average finance charge of $4,000. The loans carry substant=
ial risk, as a single missed payment triggers a default and the remainder o=
f the $30,000 =E2=80=9Ccap=E2=80=9D becomes due immediately. BloomTech furt=
her hid the cost and nature of the =E2=80=9Cincome share=E2=80=9D loans by =
not disclosing key terms like the finance charge and annual percentage rate=
, as required by law.I mean! On the one hand, yes, absolutely, as a technic=
al matter these things=C2=A0must=C2=A0be loans; there is nothing else that =
they could be. And perhaps the terms of the loans are harsh (the $30,000 ca=
p is due immediately on default?). And=C2=A0if you take the average amount =
repaid, and subtract the average amount borrowed, you can compute something=

 annual percentage rate or a range of APRs from that. And I can see how a c=
onsumer-debt regulator would say =E2=80=9Cthis is consumer debt, our consum=
er debt regulations require APR disclosures, you didn=E2=80=99t disclose an=
 APR, so pay a fine.=E2=80=9DOn the other hand =E2=80=A6=C2=A0like, it=E2=
=80=99s=C2=A0technically=C2=A0false to say these things are =E2=80=9Cnot de=
bt,=E2=80=9D but you know what they mean, right? They mean =E2=80=9Cthis th=

her than a fixed amount.=E2=80=9D (Unless you miss a payment, oops.) There =

A story that I would have told about private equity is that, in the olden d=
ays, a private equity fund was a couple of youngish ex-bankers who put up t=
heir own money and raised some money from outside investors to do leveraged=
 buyouts of companies with very small equity checks, a novel and risky mode=
l in which all the participants =E2=80=94=C2=A0the fund=E2=80=99s founders,=
 their outside investors, the lenders =E2=80=94=C2=A0were consciously takin=
g big risks in the swashbucklier corners of the financial markets. And now,=
 decades later, those private equity firms have become gigantic alternative=
 asset managers that run hundreds of billions of dollars for insurance comp=
anies, with thousands of employees and much steadier and more diversified r=
eturns. Everything has become domesticated and institutionalized. You don=
=E2=80=99t invest with a private equity firm, these days, because you like =
the fire in the eyes of its 30-year-old founder; you invest because of its =
giant platform and suite of products and long track record.Still you want a=
 little fire in the eyes. Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0Laura Benitez and Swetha=
 Gopinath report:The biggest backers of buyout firms are wielding their eve=
r-growing bargaining power to drive increasingly tough deals. The latest in=
 a string of investor demands is this: If you want our money, put more of y=
ours in first. As a result, some private equity executives =E2=80=94 at bot=
h big and small firms =E2=80=94 are loading up on more debt and pledging pe=
rsonal assets to appease them. This includes their homes and other valuable=
s as well as traditional collateral such as fees and stakes in other funds.=
The equity contributions from buyout firms into new funds has jumped to an =
average of 5% from 2% last year, and in some cases as much as 20%, accordin=
g to people familiar with the matter. It=E2=80=99s leaving some managers sc=
rambling to find significant amounts of capital in often short time frames,=
 the people said, asking not to be identified discussing private agreements=
.To raise cash, some are taking out high-interest loans that can charge rat=
es into the mid-to-high teens, and are often backed by holiday homes, cars,=
 second businesses and art collections, the people said. Some banks are dem=
anding that executives pledge the bulk, if not all of their personal assets=
, some of the people said.=E2=80=9CWe are not going to invest in your priva=
te equity fund unless you=E2=80=99d lose your house if you mess up=E2=80=9D=
 just seems like more of a 1970s ask than a 2020s one, but I like it. Meanw=
hile of course the giant alternative asset managers are also in the busines=
s of lending each other money to invest in their funds:Investec Plc and JPM=
organ Chase & Co. are among banks providing such loans, according to people=
 familiar with the matter. The industry=E2=80=99s need for more capital has=
 also given rise to several private credit lenders such as Ares Management,=
 Oaktree Capital Management=E2=80=99s 17Capital and Pemberton Asset Managem=
ent, who have raised, or are raising funds to lend out.So:=09Do you think t=
hat the Ares and Oaktree professionals need to put a lot of their personal =
assets into those lending-money-to-private-equity-people funds? Do they hav=
e to borrow to do so? From each other? Or what?=09There=E2=80=99s going to =
be an absolutely amazing Money Stuff when Oaktree tries to seize an Apollo =
managing director=E2=80=99s house after a fund does poorly.
 =20
   =20
      AI returns
   =20
 =20
If you had a superintelligent robot, how could you use it to make money? Se=
ll subscriptions to the robot? Predict stock prices and make trades? Ask it=
 to dream up products that everyone will want to buy? Hack into banks? Targ=
eted advertising? I don=E2=80=99t know, but this question has an obvious me=
ta-answer, which is: =E2=80=9CAsk the superintelligent robot what the best =
way is to make money, and then do that.=E2=80=9D Maybe it=E2=80=99s ads, I =
don=E2=80=99t know, but I bet the robot does. Anyway here=E2=80=99s a video=
 of=C2=A0Sam Altman=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0it=E2=80=99s old, but it=E2=80=99s =
making the rounds again this week =E2=80=94 telling an audience of venture =
capitalists, to somewhat nervous laughter, =E2=80=9Conce we build a general=
ly intelligent system, basically we will ask it to figure out a way to gene=
rate an investment return for you.=E2=80=9D But this is plainly the correct=
 answer! Any other answer would be wrong! =E2=80=9COoh we=E2=80=99ll sell a=
ds on the superintelligent robot,=E2=80=9D no you won=E2=80=99t, not if the=
 robot has better ideas! The whole point of the robot is to have better ide=
as.

gher-profile lawsuits about this stuff, many of which seem to be more about=
 code theft than idea theft. =E2=80=9CThese things are scary,=E2=80=9D he w=
rites, =E2=80=9Cbut the goal is keep things in a precarious equilibrium, wh=
ere people defect, but not too much. =E2=80=A6 To paraphrase @matt_levine, =
=E2=80=98Everything is IP theft.=E2=80=99 If your employer is unhappy, you =
can be a thief. Just enough to deter.=E2=80=9D


  [2] The complaint notes that Schadewald=E2=80=99s background was trading =
S&P 500 index options, variance swaps and VIX futures at Barclays Capital, =
though he =E2=80=9Chad no significant experience trading in [redacted] prio=
r to joining Jane Street,=E2=80=9D and that Spottiswood =E2=80=9Cwas a trad=
er on the options trading desk and reported to Schadewald=E2=80=9D at Jane =
Street.


  [3] The complaint also mentions another way Jane Street learned of a comp=
etitor=E2=80=99s entrance: =E2=80=9CJane Street=E2=80=99s third party broke=
r =E2=80=94 used to execute the Trading Strategy =E2=80=94 temporarily stop=
ped processing Jane Street=E2=80=99s orders on March 6 and 28, 2024, due to=
 the broker [REDACTED] =E2=80=A6 On information and belief, the new competi=
tor caused the third party broker to exceed margin limits due to the execut=
ion of [REDACTED], mirroring Jane Street=E2=80=99s Trading Strategy.=E2=80=
=9D Basically the trade has some capacity limit based on the margin limits =
of the broker Jane Street used; once Millennium allegedly got in, it used u=
p the capacity.


  [4] That=E2=80=99s not quite right; those numbers are adjusted for subseq=
uent stock splits, so that they are comparable to Tesla=E2=80=99s current s=
hare count and stock price. See page 66 of yesterday=E2=80=99s proxy statem=
ent for the actual at-the-time numbers: The quantity was =E2=80=9Capproxima=
tely 20.3 million option shares equivalent to 303,960,630 shares of our com=
mon stock today after giving effect to stock splits,=E2=80=9D and the exerc=
ise price was the =E2=80=9CFair Market Value (FMV) of Tesla common stock on=
 the grant date of January 21, 2018, which was $350.02 per share of our com=
mon stock (based on the closing price on January 19, 2018, the last trading=
 day prior to the grant date). Equivalent to $23.34 per share of our common=
 stock today after giving effect to stock splits.=E2=80=9D


  [5] I believe it comes originally from a table on page 18 of Tesla=E2=80=
=99s 2018 proxy statement describing the plan.


  [6] That is, $40 billion of intrinsic value, or value if he exercised the=
m today. (The $155.45 current stock price, minus the $23.34 strike price, t=
imes 304 million.) The Black-Scholes value is probably a bit higher but let=
=E2=80=99s not quibble; Bloomberg=E2=80=99s OV calculator tells me the delt=
a is around 99%.


  [7] Don=E2=80=99t email me to complain about the phrase =E2=80=9Cexpected=
 value,=E2=80=9D I know. Page 55 of the 2019 proxy statement gives the valu=
e Tesla attributed to the award, $2,283,988,504.


  [8] Page 123 of the Form 10-K for 2018 describes how the $2.3 billion was=
 amortized through the income statements.


  [9] The *per-share* expense to Tesla was higher because the options dilut=
e the shares, but still. And of course if the market cap had stayed below $=
100 billion, the options would have turned out to be worth $0 to Musk, but =

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One sort of thing you could do, if you=
 work at an investing firm, is say =E2=80=9CI like Microsoft Corp., I think=
 this artificial intelligence thing is big, I think the stock will go up.=
=E2=80=9D And then you buy Microsoft stock with your firm=E2=80=99s capital=
, and hopefully it goes up.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another sort of=
 thing you could do, if you work at an investing firm, is design a complex =
software system that ingests millions of data points to find predictive sig=
nals and then uses those signals to make automated low-latency trades in mu=
ltiple assets on multiple exchanges with limited human supervision. You wri=
te thousands of lines of code, you test the system, you put it into product=
ion, it runs on its own and hopefully it makes money.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"=
margin: 16px 0;">And then one day, as these things go, you might leave your=
 investing firm and go work for another one. What can you bring with you? W=
ell, if your big idea at your old job was =E2=80=9Cwe should buy Microsoft,=
=E2=80=9D and you get to your new job, and on the first day you walk in and=
 say =E2=80=9CI like Microsoft, I think this artificial intelligence thing =
is big, I think the stock will go up,=E2=80=9D then probably they will say =
=E2=80=9Cgreat, that sort of insight is exactly why we hired you, let=E2=80=
=99s buy Microsoft.=E2=80=9D And you=E2=80=99ll buy Microsoft stock with yo=
ur new firm=E2=80=99s capital. And if you then go have drinks with your for=
mer colleagues at your old firm, you might mention =E2=80=9Chey I bought so=
me Microsoft,=E2=80=9D and they=E2=80=99ll be like =E2=80=9Cyeah, you alway=
s did like Microsoft,=E2=80=9D and it=E2=80=99ll be a nice moment of remini=
scence.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Whereas if your big idea at your ol=
d job was a complex software system ingesting millions of data points, and =
before you leave your old job you email the code to your Gmail account, and=
 then you get to your new job, and on the first day you walk and and are li=
ke =E2=80=9Cgimme a sec guys, gotta Gmail myself my code so I can set it up=
 my system to do trades for us,=E2=80=9D there will be a stunned silence. I=
 mean, for one thing, your trading strategy worked on your old firm=E2=80=
=99s software platform, and if you want to run it at your new firm you=E2=
=80=99ll probably need to rewrite it for their platform. But also, that sof=
tware system you built is surely the intellectual property of your old firm=
, and if you use it at your new firm and the old firm finds out, you and yo=
ur new firm will get very sued. Even if you do rewrite it for your=C2=A0new=
 firm=E2=80=99s platform. Really, even if you never emailed it to yourself =
and just rewrite it, or approximate it, from memory. That system =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0its structure and methods and the ideas behind it =E2=80=94 probably =
does belong to your old firm.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But there are=
 a lot of other things you could do, if you work at an investment firm, tha=
t are somewhere between =E2=80=9Cbuy Microsoft=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cbuild =
a complex software system.=E2=80=9D We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/35081496.257345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcG=
luaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDMtMDcvZXh4b24tYmFyZ2VzLWluLW9uLWhlc3MtZGVhbD9jb=
XBpZD1CQkQwNDE4MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3=
c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA0MTgmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cBa87baa85" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;">talked last month</a> about a weird trade i=
n Avid Bioservices Inc.=E2=80=99s convertible bonds: Avid forgot to do a fa=
irly trivial administrative task required by its bond indenture, and as a r=
esult holders of the convertible could demand their money back, creating a =
pretty large windfall for them. If they noticed. Some holder or holders =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0I don=E2=80=99t know who, but I assume it=E2=80=99s a hedge fun=
d =E2=80=94=C2=A0noticed, bought up the bonds cheap, accelerated them and g=
ot the windfall.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you worked at tha=
t hedge fund, noticing the error and buying up those Avid bonds, and then y=
ou quit and moved to a different hedge fund, could you start buying up thos=
e Avid bonds for your=C2=A0<em>new=C2=A0</em>hedge fund? Does that count as=
 stealing the intellectual property of your old fund? The intellectual prop=
erty is something like =E2=80=9Cwe read this indenture and noticed a glitch=
.=E2=80=9D But the indenture is public, the glitch is public, the strategy =
is just noticing it. It seems weird to me=C2=A0to think that your old firm =
could sue you, saying =E2=80=9CAvid=E2=80=99s mistake is our intellectual p=
roperty and you=E2=80=99re not allowed to use it.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">That is, the <em>essential </em>sort of thing that you do, =
at an investing firm =E2=80=94=C2=A0the meta-task that everyone does =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0is identify market anomalies that can make you money. In the most =
general case, it is not clear how those anomalies could=C2=A0<em>belong=C2=
=A0</em>to your firm.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But I don=E2=80=99t k=
now, that=E2=80=99s not legal advice.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#f=
ootnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; borde=
r-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: non=
e !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-blo=
ck;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0In practice the way that hedge funds often address this is with n=
on-compete agreements and gardening leave: If you leave your old firm, you =
are often contractually not allowed to start at your new one for some perio=
d of months or years. And in that time, the trades and strategies that you =
worked on at your old firm will become stale, so by the time you get to you=
r new firm you probably won=E2=80=99t be able to replicate, or mess up, the=
 trades your old firm is now doing.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last we=
ek, Bloomberg=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/35081496.257345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzL=
zIwMjQtMDQtMTIvamFuZS1zdHJlZXQtYWNjdXNlcy1taWxsZW5uaXVtLWV4LXRyYWRlcnMtb2Yt=
c3RlYWxpbmctc3RyYXRlZ3k_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQxODI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1=
lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwNDE4JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj=
1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3527a920" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" it=
emscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decor=
ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Chris Dolmetsch r=
eported</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Jane Street Group =
sued Millennium Management and two former traders over their alleged theft =
of a highly confidential and =E2=80=9Cimmensely valuable=E2=80=9D proprieta=
ry trading strategy.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a complaint filed F=
riday in federal court in Manhattan, Jane Street said former traders Dougla=
s Schadewald and Daniel Spottiswood, who resigned earlier this year to join=
 Millennium, had been =E2=80=9Cintimately involved=E2=80=9D in the developm=
ent of the strategy. According to the suit, they are now using it at Izzy E=
nglander=E2=80=99s hedge fund group in violation of confidentiality agreeme=
nts.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35081496.257345/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHM=
uYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JFWFZqRmZCVW5uNC92MA/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc95a2823" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">the complaint</a>, which is redacte=
d just enough to be tantalizing. Starting in about 2018, =E2=80=9CJane Stre=
et deployed significant resources=E2=80=9D to develop trading strategies fo=
r, uh, [redacted]. I=C2=A0guess it has something to do with listed stock op=
tions in some non-US market.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2=
" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-=
width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !import=
ant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a> What the researchers found shocked them:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">The understanding and insights that resulted from this analy=
sis and research, as well as the magnitude of the potential opportunities, =
were counterintuitive, unexpected, and initially met with significant skept=
icism and incredulity internally at Jane Street. However, the results of Ja=
ne Street=E2=80=99s analysis and research indicated there was tremendous po=
tential opportunity if the trading concepts could be further developed and =
applied.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In 2023, they conduct=
ed an experiment and liked the results:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">Jane Street risked substantial capital in trading activity to bett=
er understand (and verify) the tremendous opportunity presented by the Trad=
ing Strategy. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The analysis of the resul=
ts of the 2023 Trading Investigation confirmed Jane Street=E2=80=99s initia=
l findings =E2=80=94=C2=A0notwithstanding their facial improbability =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0and revealed that the market was [redacted] for certain classes of=
 trades in the presence of specific market signals and underlying condition=
s.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Through the 2023 Trading Investigation, =
Jane Street identified certain signals and strategies, and methods of analy=
zing and interpreting those signals, that enabled Jane Street to reliably p=
redict future market activity. Jane Street could then trade profitably base=
d on these predictions.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
So they put it into production and made buckets of money, while continuing =
to find it weird:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">During his ti=
me at Jane Street, Schadewald stated that the Trading Strategy was =E2=80=
=9Cincredibly valuable,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Csuper valuable,=E2=80=9D and cont=
inually emphasized to leadership =E2=80=9Chow valuable the trading is.=E2=
=80=9D Schadewald also repeatedly stressed how unusual and counterintuitive=
 the Trading Strategy was. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While at Jan=
e Street, Spottiswood acknowledged how valuable and counterintuitive the Tr=
ading Strategy was, saying he was =E2=80=9Cshocked=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Csu=
rprised=E2=80=9D by its results, and that it was =E2=80=9Creally awesome.=
=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was presumably =
good for the traders =E2=80=94=C2=A0including Schadewald and Spottiswood =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0who were doing the trade. But, says the complaint, there was=
 a way for them to do better: Shop their skills elsewhere. In February, Sch=
adewald and Spottiswood left Jane Street for Millennium. They apparently go=
t a good deal:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Based on convers=
ations between Schadewald and his colleagues at Jane Street, Schadewald=E2=
=80=99s compensation from Millennium [redacted].</p><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">Schadewald=E2=80=99s compensation at Millennium is atypical and above=
-market as compared to a competitor=E2=80=99s typical arm=E2=80=99s length =
offer to a trader with Schadewald=E2=80=99s experience and performance.</p>=
</blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In general, it is a good sign for=
 you if your compensation is =E2=80=9C[redacted].=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9C[Redact=
ed]=E2=80=9D is not a higher amount of compensation than=C2=A0<em>any </em>=
determinate number, but it=E2=80=99s pretty good.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">The complaint says that Schadewald was hired to run a =E2=80=
=9Cpod=E2=80=9D=C2=A0at Millennium, with its own allocation of capital and =
its own trading strategy. In general, multi-strategy =E2=80=9Cpod shops=E2=
=80=9D like Millennium are always looking for new strategies that are not c=
orrelated with their existing strategies. Jane Street thinks that Schadewal=
d=E2=80=99s pod is doing the secret strategy developed at Jane Street<a cla=
ss=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent=
 !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Despite the profitability=
 of the Trading Strategy, Jane Street is aware of no competitor which has i=
mplemented a similar strategy =E2=80=94=C2=A0until Defendants did. Due to m=
arket dynamics and constraints, the entrance of a large competitor employin=
g the same strategy at scale could not go unnoticed. ...</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">Within days of Schadewald=E2=80=99s joining Millennium, Jane =
Street=E2=80=99s profits attributable to the Trading Strategy decreased sub=
stantially, and the decrease accelerated in the period following Spottiswoo=
d=E2=80=99s start at Millennium.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">Unusually for the industry, they had no non-compete or gardening l=
eave: =E2=80=9CAs a matter of [its] collaborative culture,=E2=80=9D the com=
plaint says, =E2=80=9CJane Street does not typically require employees to s=
ign non-compete agreements.=E2=80=9D (Apparently working at Jane Street is =
so nice and lucrative that people don=E2=80=99t traditionally leave for com=
petitors?) And:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While it is typ=
ical for traders transitioning between firms to take some period of time of=
f, on information and belief, Schadewald and Spottiswood started at Millenn=
ium almost immediately.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean=
 =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I wouldn=E2=80=99t have said =E2=80=9Cit is typical for tra=
ders transitioning between firms to take some period of time off=E2=80=9D? =
It=E2=80=99s not, like, a nice social custom. I would have said =E2=80=9Cit=
 is typical for firms to have non-compete agreements that=C2=A0<em>force=C2=
=A0</em>traders to take time off if they=E2=80=99re leaving for another fir=
m.=E2=80=9D Firms force traders to take time off so that their firm-specifi=
c trade knowledge has some time to decay. If you=E2=80=99re free to start t=
rading the minute you leave, maybe you will.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">Anyway, Millennium started doing whatever it is doing, Jane Street's prof=
its suffered, and it sent Millennium a letter saying =E2=80=9Cknock it off.=
=E2=80=9D Millennium declined; the complaint says that =E2=80=9CSchadewald =
and Spottiswood claim they =E2=80=98are entitled to put their own hard-earn=
ed skill and ability to work at [Millennium].=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know! Jane Street found a market=
 anomaly, and made a lot of money trading it. Schadewald and Spottiswood =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0who traded on the anomaly at Jane Street =E2=80=94=C2=A0then=
 took the anomaly to Millennium. What they took with them was apparently so=
me sort of=C2=A0<em>strategy=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0some way to reliably =
make money by trading in the [redacted] market =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it doesn=
=E2=80=99t seem to have been an especially complex system.=C2=A0You can tel=
l in part because of how quickly they allegedly spun it up at Millennium: S=
chadewald left Jane Street on Feb. 7, and Jane Street=E2=80=99s profits wer=
e crushed by March. This sounds less like =E2=80=9CJane Street built an aut=
omated system to identify subtle market signals that they took to their new=
 job,=E2=80=9D and more like =E2=80=9CJane Street noticed that Ruritanian e=
quity index options were cheap on Thursdays, and they went to Millennium to=
 buy those options on Thursdays.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I=
s that allowed? Schadewald and Spottiswood had nondisclosure/intellectual-p=
roperty agreements while working at Jane Street; the complaint quotes them:=
</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Schadewald agreed that =E2=80=
=9Call intellectual property, including trade secrets, [] discoveries, conc=
epts, [] know-how, [] models, methods, techniques, [and] processes [] =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A0shall belong to [Jane Street].=E2=80=9D ...</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">Confidential Information does not include =E2=80=9Cinformation=
, knowledge or data that constitutes general skills, knowledge and experien=
ce,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cinformation known by the Undersigned prior to the Und=
ersigned=E2=80=99s employment,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cinformation publicly av=
ailable, generally known or readily ascertainable from public sources,=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0<em>however,=C2=A0</em>the Schadewald IP Agreement required tha=
t Schadewald acknowledge that a =E2=80=9Ccombination, integration or compil=
ation of such information, either with or using [] Confidential Information=
,=C2=A0<em>is itself [] Confidential Information</em>.=E2=80=9D</p></blockq=
uote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Was this trade =E2=80=9Creadily ascertain=
able from public sources=E2=80=9D? Was it part of the general skills that t=
hey picked up at Jane Street and then brought to other jobs? Or was it a di=
scovery,=C2=A0method, technique, etc., that is property of Jane Street? Jan=
e Street invested a lot of time and effort into finding a market anomaly, a=
nd that anomaly was, it emphasizes, really weird and counterintuitive. Does=
 that mean that Jane Street owns it?</p><table class=3D"liveintent-sponsore=

5px;">Elon=E2=80=99s money</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is customary to say that, in 2018, =
Tesla Inc. gave Elon Musk a compensation package worth as much as $55.8 bil=
lion. What it actually gave him was a=C2=A0series of options to buy about 3=
04 million shares of Tesla stock for $23.34 each,<a class=3D"footnote-index=
" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display=
: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0but only if he met certain performance goals over the 10-year ter=
m of his pay plan,=C2=A0mainly taking Tesla=E2=80=99s market capitalization=
 from about $59 billion (at the time he got the options) to $650 billion. H=
e accomplished those goals within three years, so he got all the options.</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The $55.8 billon number is pretty arbitrary:=
 It represents how much the options=C2=A0<em>would=C2=A0</em>be worth at a =
Tesla market cap of=C2=A0<em>exactly=C2=A0</em>$650 billion.<a class=3D"foo=
tnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration:=
 none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; b=
order-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !importa=
nt; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a> In the event, Tesla=E2=80=99s market cap got as high as $1.2 trillion =
in 2021, at which point Musk=E2=80=99s option package was worth more than $=
100 billion. Tesla=E2=80=99s stock closed yesterday at $155.45, for a marke=
t cap of about $495 billion, making the options worth something like $40 bi=
llion.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000=
000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bot=
tom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color:=
 transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But at the time they were granted, in =
2018, Musk could not extract any money at all from them. He=E2=80=99d only =
be able to exercise them if he hit the performance targets, which he hadn=
=E2=80=99t yet. And because the $23.34 strike price of the options was set =
to be the same as Tesla=E2=80=99s stock price at the time, even exercising =
the options, in 2018, wouldn=E2=80=99t make him a profit: He=E2=80=99d pay =
$23.34 to get $23.34 worth of stock, which he could just do in the open mar=
ket. The options were worth $0 if=C2=A0Tesla maintained the status quo; the=
y=E2=80=99d only be worth anything to Musk if it grew a lot.</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">But the options weren=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>worth zero</em>,=
 as an economic matter: There was some probability that Tesla=C2=A0<em>woul=
d=C2=A0</em>grow and he=E2=80=99d get to exercise the options and make $55.=
8 billion, or more, or less. Finance has reasonably well-understood ways to=
 put a single current numerical value=C2=A0on=C2=A0this uncertain distribut=
ion of potential future values. Tesla determined that the options were wort=
h about $2.3 billion at the time Musk got them: There was some chance they=
=E2=80=99d end up worthless, some chance they=E2=80=99d end up worth $55.8 =
billion, some chance they=E2=80=99d end up worth $100 billion or $40 billio=
n or any other nonnegative number, but, averaging over all those possibilit=
ies, the expected value was $2.3 billion.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If Tesla gave Musk a thing worth $2.3 =
billion, that was an expense to Tesla, which reduced its net income. And so=
 Tesla=E2=80=99s income statements reflected that $2.3 billion expense over=
 the period of Musk=E2=80=99s pay plan.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"=
#footnote-8" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: n=
one !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-b=
lock;">
<span>[8]</span>
</a></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Notice that Tesla had an expense of $2=
.3 billion, while Musk got options that turned out to be worth more than $1=
00 billion at their peak. That=E2=80=99s a nice trade, a nice feature of st=
ock-options-based compensation: The expense to Tesla turned out to be much =
lower than the actual value delivered to Musk.<a class=3D"footnote-index" h=
ref=3D"#footnote-9" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: i=
nline-block;">
<span>[9]</span>
</a>=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway, <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35081496.257345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZX=
JnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMzEvZWxvbi1tdXNrLWlzLW92ZXJwYWlkP=
2NtcGlkPUJCRDA0MTgyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1u=
ZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDQxOCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cB7d24b55f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" t=
arget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: solid; background-color: #ccc;">this January a Delaware judge decided</a=
> that Musk=E2=80=99s 2018 pay package was illegally big, and decreed that =
the options would vanish. And yesterday, Tesla asked its shareholders to re=
-approve the options, so that they will=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>vanish. We <=
